body_txt: Nowadays, you have to expect the bride and groom to object to an Arranged Marriage . Commit for life to someone they might not like, or even know? No way. They'll tear their clothes, gnash their teeth, lock themselves in their rooms, and swear that they'll never, ever — is that them necking in the atrium? Sometimes the couple who have been so strenuously unhappy about their betrothal discover they not only like but love each other, and it is perfectly clear that they would have paired up even if a third party hadn't put them together. Drama being what it is , you can expect one of their initial plans to undo or back out of the arranged marriage to then succeed , and their parents will set them up with a new betrothed that they truly can't stand. Expect one or both to have to swallow their pride and come out and say they want their parents' first offer after all. A variant is that both meet outside of the home environment (before or after the declaration) without recognizing each other . Maybe they ran away from home entirely, only to happily embrace "a fellow in misery" — and later commiserate about their bossy parents. Eventually, once they recognize one another, their common experience helps them fall in love. Sometimes this perfectly arranged marriage doesn't come about randomly, but intentionally by parents, like a Batman Gambit scenario. One or more of the parents involved who knows both well enough has arranged the marriage since both are highly compatible and could naturally fall in love. In fact, this is the purpose of an 'arranged marriage' in the first place. It is akin to a matchmaking service, and the couple will generally have some sort of courtship before tying the knot, and it's very rare for someone to be forced to marry a person they despise. Even a Gold Digger would want a decent relationship if for no other reason than to help in securing the knot. In other cultures, however, especially feudal societies, marriages would be struck between noble houses to secure alliances, expand territory, bring the family closer to the throne, etc . In cases like this, the couple's individual romantic tastes are a very low priority, as they see the marriage as a duty to their families. But then it turns out they love each other after all. This trope is frequently used as a justification for the use of the Arranged Marriage and Altar Diplomacy tropes to audiences with modern sensibilities, who are often uncomfortable with the concept of forced marriage. It's not a violation of free will if both want to get married, after all. Compare Marriage Before Romance for when a couple has gone through with a marriage for other reasons than love and only then starts to develop feelings for one another. Expect a lot of arguing and bickering to contrast with the more idealised love story of the unwed, ending in a big Aw, Look! They Really Do Love Each Other moment, to the surprise of everyone, most of all themselves. Can go hand-and-hand with Childhood Friend Romance if the betrothed pair grew up together.
